query: does immediate breast reconstruction affect the delivery of adjuvant chemotherapy

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Reconstruction -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Immediate repair refers to a reconstruction performed during the same surgical session as the cancer resection. While this prolongs the time under anesthesia, the advantage of using a more natural skin envelope for reconstructive options is considerable. The skin envelope created during skin/nipple-sparing mastectomy can leave the patient with a more natural-appearing breast postoperatively. Oncologic considerations such as stage and BRCA mutation status are key in determining if this approach is reasonable. Immediate reconstruction is affected by the need for adjuvant therapy, specifically radiation therapy. The risk of wound breakdown and other complications is significantly increased when a foreign body, such as a breast implant, is within the radiated field. [7] Patients requiring adjuvant radiation are often best served with autologous tissue reconstruction, whether pedicled or free flap in nature. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Reconstruction -- Complications -- Immediate
retrieved_docs.content: Bleeding Edema Tissue necrosis Moderate-to-severe pain Breast asymmetry

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Reconstruction Expander Implant -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: During their initial consultations, patients should be educated on all appropriate reconstructive options, along with their risks and benefits. Many patients may benefit from referral to survivorship group therapies before their procedure to gain insight into what to expect postoperatively and hear individual patient experiences with various techniques. Antibiotics should be given during the immediate preoperative period to decrease the risk of the skin flora contaminating the wound.

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Reconstruction -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Distinguish between immediate and delayed surgical options for breast reconstructive surgery. Identify the absolute and relative contraindications to breast reconstruction. Appraise the indications, risks, and benefits of different surgical approaches used in breast reconstruction. Apply effective healthcare team processes for coordinated prehospital and hospital care to improve clinical outcomes for patients undergoing breast reconstruction in the setting of a breast cancer diagnosis. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Reconstruction Free Flaps -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the different muscle flaps used to reconstruct the breast. Outline the technique of the TRAM flap. Summarize the indications for muscle flaps to reconstruct the breast. Describe the importance of improving care coordination among the interprofessional team to enhance the delivery of care for patients undergoing breast reconstruction with muscle flaps. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Cancer Conservation Therapy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify Implthe indications for breast conservation therapy in the management of early-stage breast cancer. Determine the preoperative workup, operative steps, and post-operative follow-up for partial mastectomy with axillary lymph node mapping. Identify the most common adverse events following breast conservation therapy and associated risk factors. Implement interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance breast-conserving therapy and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Postmastectomy Breast Cancer Radiation Therapy -- Treatment / Management -- Internal mammary node coverage
retrieved_docs.content: Postmastectomy radiation therapy after breast reconstruction has been associated with increased rates of capsular contracture, reconstructive failures, revision surgeries, and overall worse cosmetic outcomes [19] . The timing of the breast reconstruction (ie, immediate versus delayed) and type of breast reconstruction (ie, autologous versus implant-based) are important considerations that require an informative discussion with the patient and multi-disciplinary team before surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Reconstruction -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Breast cancer is the most common cancer and the second leading cause of cancer death in women in the United States. Breast cancer treatment has progressively improved with advances in endocrine therapy, early detection, and breast conservation surgical techniques. However, for patients undergoing mastectomy, the concerns of a potentially disfiguring surgery after a cancer diagnosis significantly affect the patient’s treatment and overall psychosocial recovery. The plastic surgeon's goal, along with that of the patient and her breast surgeon, is to develop a plan to restore body image after successful oncologic treatment. Breast reconstruction is multifaceted; chemoradiation, procedure choice, timing, and the entire scope of the cancer treatment must be considered before proceeding with reconstruction. Breast reconstruction surgery can restore self-confidence and eliminate the need for uncomfortable prosthetics. This activity reviews commonly used breast reconstruction options, describing each approach's potential indications, contraindications, risks, and benefits. The role of the healthcare team in managing and improving care for patients who undergo breast reconstructive surgery is discussed.

retrieved_docs.title: Unipedicled Transverse Rectus Abdominis Myocutaneous (TRAM) Flap Breast Reconstruction -- Preparation -- Oncologic Coordination and Timing
retrieved_docs.content: Timing (immediate vs delayed reconstruction) is tailored to the oncologic plan and patient preference. For patients expected to receive adjuvant radiation, options include: Immediate reconstruction with a larger flap to accommodate anticipated radiation-induced volume loss. Delayed reconstruction after radiation therapy to optimize flap quality and reduce complication risks. Close collaboration with breast surgeons helps determine the mastectomy type (eg, skin-sparing vs nipple-sparing) and guides flap planning accordingly.

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperatively Adjustable Breast Implant -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The skills involved in post-mastectomy reconstruction with postoperative adjustable implants require specific technique and expertise, which takes time to develop. The strategy depends on the approach, whether concomitant mastectomy reconstruction versus delayed reconstruction is decided upon. It is important to educate the nursing staff to monitor flap integrity postoperatively. In the preoperative preparation for any of the reconstructions, it is very crucial to educate the patient on smoking cessation. Postoperatively, patient education on drain care, diabetes control as well as shoulder range of motion exercises play an important role in the recovery stage.

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Reconstruction -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: A significant consideration during the planning process is the timing of reconstructive procedures. Both immediate repair and delayed repair are options. The decision must be tailored to each patient and their specific oncologic situation to ensure optimum safety, oncologic soundness, and final functional and aesthetic outcome.

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Reconstruction -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Delayed reconstruction is when a patient has a planned mastectomy, then returns to the operating room a second time to perform the reconstructive portion of the procedure. This option is available for patients who may not have decided whether to have breast reconstruction or require adjuvant radiation therapy to optimize outcomes. Breast restoration utilizing alloplastic implants is discussed in a subsequent article and remains an option for many patients in immediate and delayed scenarios. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Reconstruction Free Flaps -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: A thorough history and physical is requisite. Risks and comorbidities are reviewed and modified when appropriate; this requires discussing the plan with anesthesia, especially avoiding bands, IVs, and blood pressure cuffs on donor extremities, and the avoidance of pressors and vasoactive drugs during the case, and the need to have anticoagulants available.

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Reconstruction Free Flaps -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Plastic surgeons are always involved in breast reconstruction.  However, they must work very closely with the oncologic breast surgeon to determine the best treatment path for the patient.  For free flaps, the skin incision/excision is typically planned by both teams so that the resection can be oncologic, but also as cosmetically sensitive as possible. Other specialists including radiation oncologists and medical oncologists are also part of the patient's care team and plans for chemotherapy and radiation impact reconstruction timing and choice.  Patients with very complex diagnoses are frequently discussed at an interprofessional tumor board to determine their best options.  It is also necessary to have an excellent nursing team who are specialty trained to monitor free flaps and can alert the plastic surgeon immediately if there is a problem.  The interprofessional approach is the best means by which to increase optimal patient care and satisfaction, as well as minimizing risks and complications. [Level V]

retrieved_docs.title: Breast Reconstruction Perforator Flaps -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the indications of a perforator flap. Describe the advantages of a perforator flap. Summarize the complications of a perforator flap. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Nipple-Areolar Complex Reconstruction -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Correctly timing NAC reconstruction is imperative for optimizing outcomes. The type of reconstruction and the need for adjuvant treatment must be considered. NAC reconstruction should only be undertaken after achieving a stable breast mound, typically 3 months after autologous breast reconstruction or permanent implant placement in prosthetic-based reconstruction. [11] NAC reconstruction should be delayed until chemotherapy and radiation treatments are completed. Some surgeons advise against NAC reconstruction in the radiated breast and recommend NAC tattooing to improve cosmesis. Performing NAC reconstruction too early can lead to inappropriate NAC positioning, ruining an otherwise superb result. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2558
retrieved_docs.content: The breast part functions as the direct site for a range of other or therapeutic procedures on skin and breast, indicating that it is the anatomical location where such interventions are performed. Among these interventions, the excision of aberrant tissue of breast is specifically linked to the category of other or therapeutic procedures on skin and breast, highlighting that removal of abnormal breast tissue is one of the therapeutic actions carried out at the breast part. This relationship underscores the role of the breast part as the focal point for both general skin‑breast therapeutic procedures and the specific surgical removal of atypical breast tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2478
retrieved_docs.content: The female breast is the anatomical structure that may be treated with surgical procedures such as lumpectomy and quadrantectomy of the breast; both are breast‑conserving operations used to excise localized lesions while preserving as much normal breast tissue as possible, and they are commonly employed in the management of breast cancer in women.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2863
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on breast and breast: excisions are both classified as subcategories of mastectomy, indicating that mastectomy serves as the parent operation that encompasses these specific breast surgical interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 897
retrieved_docs.content: The female breast is the anatomical site that undergoes both diagnostic and therapeutic procedures. Breast biopsy and other diagnostic interventions are performed on the female breast to obtain tissue samples and assess pathology. In addition, the female breast is the target of surgical treatments such as lumpectomy and quadrantectomy, which are procedures used to remove localized breast lesions while preserving as much normal tissue as possible. These relationships underscore the continuum of care for breast conditions, linking diagnostic evaluation with appropriate surgical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1267
retrieved_docs.content: The female breast is the anatomical site for a range of diagnostic and surgical interventions. Breast biopsy and other diagnostic procedures on breast are performed on the female breast to obtain tissue samples for evaluation, and this category specifically includes the procedure referred to in Arabic as خزعة الثدي. In addition, surgical treatments such as lumpectomy and quadrantectomy of breast are directly related to the female breast, aiming to excise localized lesions while preserving as much normal tissue as possible. Together, these diagnostic and therapeutic procedures constitute essential components of breast disease assessment and management, enabling accurate diagnosis and appropriate treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2195
retrieved_docs.content: The female breast serves as the anatomical site where breast cancer manifests, with “cancer of breast” identified as a finding located in this tissue. A specific manifestation of this disease is the primary malignant neoplasm of the areola of the female breast, which is classified as a type of breast cancer. Therapeutic management of breast cancer frequently involves breast‑conserving surgical procedures performed on the female breast, such as lumpectomy and quadrantectomy, which are directly related to the organ and aim to remove malignant tissue while preserving as much normal breast tissue as possible. This interconnected knowledge underscores the relationship between the site (female breast), the disease (cancer of breast and its areolar subtype), and the standard surgical interventions (lumpectomy; quadrantectomy of breast) used in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1927
retrieved_docs.content: The female breast is the anatomical location where cancer of breast is identified, serving as the primary finding site for this malignancy. In the management of breast cancer, the female breast is also the target organ for breast‑conserving surgical procedures, specifically lumpectomy and quadrantectomy of breast, which aim to remove cancerous tissue while preserving as much normal breast tissue as possible.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: اللمفاوية الجذري) are specific modalities within the same therapeutic hierarchy. All mastectomy variants are members of both the malignant breast tumor SMQ and the set of therapeutic and diagnostic procedures for malignant diseases, and they are linked by the “completely_excised_anatomy_has_procedure” relationship to the anatomical structures they remove. Overall, the knowledge network connects diagnostic biopsies of the areola and nipple, the role of these breast parts as sites for cancer detection, and a hierarchical spectrum of surgical interventions—from targeted excisions of accessory or aberrant tissue to partial and total mastectomy with lymph‑node removal—under a unified framework of therapeutic procedures for malignant breast disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2389
retrieved_docs.content: The breast part serves as the direct site for other or therapeutic procedures on skin and breast, and within this category the procedure إزالة الحلمة الإضافية (removal of an accessory nipple) is identified as a primary mapped intervention. This indicates that interventions performed on the breast part, such as the removal of an accessory nipple, are classified under the broader group of therapeutic skin and breast procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1171

Overall, the hierarchy demonstrates that both استئصال جزئي للثدي and mastectomy are integral components of breast surgical care, each connected to the human mammary gland, to the management of malignant breast tumors, and to the broader collection of oncologic therapeutic and diagnostic procedures, with mastectomy further encompassing استئصال الثدي تحت الجلدي as a specialized technique.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2708
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on breast and breast: excisions are both classified as subcategories of the parent procedure استئصال جزئي للثدي, which denotes a partial mastectomy. This relationship indicates that these breast‑related surgical interventions fall under the broader category of partial breast removal, helping to organize surgical taxonomy and guide patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 168
retrieved_docs.content: complete removal of breast tissue. Partial mastectomy (استئصال جزئي للثدي) is a parent procedure for lumpectomy and quadrantectomy of the breast, representing removal of a breast mass without further specification (إزالة كتلة من الثدي، غير محدد بخلاف ذلك) and is linked to the anatomical sites of the mammary gland. Total mastectomy is a broader excisional procedure that completely removes the mammary gland and breast tissue; it is a child of bilateral mastectomy (استئصال ثديي) and a parent of sub‑cutaneous mastectomy (استئصال الثدي تحت الجلدي), which in turn corresponds to the generic action “removal of a breast mass, unspecified otherwise.” Complete excision also encompasses root lymph‑node excision (استئصال العقد اللمفاوية الجذري). Procedures grouped under “other or therapeutic procedures on skin and breast” include removal of accessory nipple (إزالة الحلمة الإضافية), excision of aberrant breast tissue, and excision of accessory breast, all of which are related to partial and total

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 1047
retrieved_docs.content: The female breast is the anatomical site where cancer of breast can arise, and this disease is classified in Arabic as سرطان الثدي. Management of cancer of breast frequently involves breast‑conserving surgical procedures such as lumpectomy and quadrantectomy of breast, which are directly related to the female breast. Recognizing the female breast as the finding site of breast cancer and understanding its associated surgical options are essential for accurate diagnosis, culturally appropriate communication, and effective patient care.

Collectively, these procedures demonstrate that Excision functions as a versatile operative strategy adaptable to endocrine, hepatobiliary, neurosurgical, spinal, musculoskeletal, urologic, breast, and craniofacial contexts, supporting comprehensive patient assessment, accurate coding, and optimized multidisciplinary care.
